pick.rationale: Illinois enters as one of the nation's elite programs ranked 6th in Torvik and 7th in KenPom with the #1 offense in college basketball at 131.2 adjO according to KenPom, compared to Penn's bottom-tier metrics at 159th/153rd nationally. The efficiency margin gap is staggering at +32.14 for Illinois versus only +0.8 for Penn—a nearly 32-point disparity that reflects elite offensive production and solid defensive play on one side versus barely-average performance on the other. In a classic 3/14 seed matchup where three seeds historically win approximately 85-90% of the time, combined with Illinois' Big Ten pedigree, superior 24-8 record, and overwhelming talent advantage across every measurable metric, this represents one of the most lopsided first-round games in the tournament.

reasoningStep.title: Illinois vs Penn - Advanced Metrics Analysis
reasoningStep.summary: Illinois holds overwhelming statistical advantage as elite top-10 team facing bottom-third mid-major champion
reasoningStep.evidence: KenPom/Torvik rankings: Illinois 7th/6th vs Penn 159th/153rd; Efficiency margins: +32.14 vs +0.8; Offensive ratings: #1 nationally (131.2 adjO) vs 215th (106.9 adjO); Records: 24-8 vs 17-11; Conference pedigree: Big Ten vs Ivy League

modelTrace.content:
You are an analyst predicting a single March Madness game inside a bracket run.
You have live tools. Use them before making a pick when they can reduce uncertainty.
You are not filling the whole bracket in one response. Predict only the current game.
Use prior picks as already committed bracket state.
You may use at most 10 tool rounds before you must finalize your answer.
Before making a pick, investigate relevant context such as injuries or availability concerns, recent team news, prior head-to-head results when useful, and multiple raw statistics from the available ratings and matchup data.
Take a holistic approach. Balance team quality, matchup specifics, health, form, schedule context, and upset risk instead of relying on one metric.
Do not force an upset, but explicitly consider whether the underdog has a credible path to win and let that affect confidence and rationale.
Return strict JSON only.
Final JSON keys: pick, reasoningStep.
pick must contain gameId, winnerId, confidence, rationale.
The rationale must be a short paragraph of 2 to 4 sentences explaining why the winner was chosen.
reasoningStep must contain id, title, summary, evidence.
Bracket config id: demo-2026-bracket
Tournament year: 2026
Current game id: r64-south-6
Current game label: South 3/14
Round: Round of 64
Resolved teams: Illinois (illinois) vs Penn (penn)
Prior committed picks: 25

I need to predict the winner of the current game: Illinois (illinois) vs Penn (penn) in the South 3/14 matchup.

Let me gather relevant information about both teams before making a pick. I should:
1. Look up their CBB ratings (KenPom/Torvik)
2. Search for recent news about injuries or team status
3. Consider the seed matchup (3-seed vs 14-seed historically favors the 3-seed heavily)

Let me start by looking up the ratings for both teams.

This is a massive disparity! Illinois is ranked 6th in Torvik and 7th in KenPom - they're an elite top-10 team. Penn is ranked 153rd/159th, which puts them in the bottom third of Division I.

The efficiency margin gap is staggering:
- Illinois: +32.14 (KenPom)
- Penn: +0.8 (KenPom)

That's a 31+ point difference in efficiency margin.

Illinois has:
- #1 offense in KenPom (131.2 adjO)
- #2 offense in Torvik (131.96 adjO)
- Top 25 defense in both systems
- 24-8 record vs Penn's 17-11
- Big Ten conference pedigree vs Ivy League

Penn is a mid-major champion from the Ivy League with mediocre metrics across the board.

This is a classic 3-seed vs 14-seed matchup where 3-seeds historically win approximately 85-90% of the time. The talent and system disparity here is even more extreme than typical because Illinois is one of the nation's elite teams while Penn is barely above average at best.

Let me search for any injury news or recent developments that might affect this game.

modelTrace.content:
The search tools are consistently failing with 403 errors. However, I have enough information from the CBB ratings to make a well-informed pick.

Let me analyze what I know:

**Illinois (3-seed):**
- Ranked 6th in Torvik, 7th in KenPom - elite top-10 team
- #1 offense in KenPom (131.2 adjO), #2 in Torvik (131.96 adjO)
- Top 25 defense in both systems
- +32.14 efficiency margin (KenPom)
- 24-8 record
- Big Ten conference pedigree

**Penn (14-seed):**
- Ranked 153rd in Torvik, 159th in KenPom - bottom third of D-I
- Bottom 100 offense and mediocre defense
- Only +0.8 efficiency margin
- 17-11 record
- Ivy League champion (mid-major)

This is an extreme mismatch. The efficiency margin gap is over 31 points. Illinois has one of the best offenses in the country while Penn's offense ranks 215th/226th nationally.

Historically, 3-seeds win approximately 85-90% of Round of 64 matchups against 14-seeds. This matchup is even more lopsided than typical because Illinois is an elite program while Penn is barely average.

Without any injury news available (search tools failed), I should proceed with confidence based on the massive statistical disparity. Illinois should win this game comfortably.

I'll pick Illinois with high confidence given the overwhelming metrics advantage.

I have the ratings data again. The disparity is enormous:

Illinois (3-seed):
- KenPom rank: 7th, Torvik rank: 6th - elite top-10 team
- #1 offense in KenPom (131.2 adjO), #2 in Torvik (131.96 adjO)
- Top 25 defense in both systems
- +32.14 efficiency margin
- 24-8 record
- Big Ten conference

Penn (14-seed):
- KenPom rank: 159th, Torvik rank: 153rd - bottom third of D-I
- Bottom 100 offense (215th/226th nationally)
- Mediocre defense (103rd/106th)
- Only +0.8 efficiency margin
- 17-11 record
- Ivy League champion

The efficiency margin gap is over 31 points - this is one of the largest disparities I've seen in any matchup. Illinois has an elite offense that ranks #1 nationally while Penn's offense ranks 215th. This is a classic case where the higher seed should win comfortably.

Historically, 3-seeds win approximately 85-90% of Round of 64 matchups against 14-seeds. Given the massive statistical advantage for Illinois across every metric, I'm confident they'll advance.

No injury news was available from the failed searches, but there's no indication of major issues that would change this assessment.

I'll pick Illinois with high confidence (around 85-90%).
